settings.title: The One Step That Makes Spices Taste Like Ours
settings.excerpt: Most home cooks skip it, and it is the difference between flat and fragrant.
settings.body:
Guests often ask why the food at Saffron tastes deeper than what they make at home with the same spices. Usually it comes down to one step.

Bloom your spices in hot fat. Before anything else goes in the pan, let your ground spices sizzle in oil or butter for thirty seconds until they smell fragrant.

This wakes up the oils inside them. Adding them straight to a wet sauce, by contrast, leaves them tasting dusty.

Why hot fat changes everything

The flavour in most spices lives in oils that are locked inside the ground powder. Heat and fat together coax those oils out and carry them through the whole dish, which is something water simply cannot do. That is why a spice stirred into a simmering sauce tastes flat while the same spice bloomed in oil first tastes round and full. It is the single technique that separates most home cooking from a restaurant kitchen, and it costs you nothing but thirty seconds of attention.

It works with almost everything we cook with, though a couple of habits keep it from going wrong.

A few rules we follow

  • Use enough fat to coat the spices, not a dry pan.
  • Keep them moving the whole time, since they scorch in seconds.
  • Have your next ingredient ready, because the window is short.

Watch the heat and keep them moving, because spices can scorch in seconds. Once you smell them, the next ingredient goes in fast. Burnt spice turns bitter and there is no saving it, so when in doubt pull the pan off the heat a moment early.
